HEALTH & SAFETY GUIDELINES FOR CONTRACTORS  4  •   Only those persons who are medically fit and have no phobia fo r working at height should be  engaged by the Contractor/Sub - contractors for such jobs.  •   The contractor shall conduct   toolbox   talk every day to all the workers and records should be  maintained and kept the document ready during the time of checking/Audit.  •   T he contractor shall ensure that their workers only perform designated work in designated  location.  •   The contractor shall not appoint sub - contractor without the written permission from HIL. Safety  code of conduct of HIL will also be applicable to the sub - con tractor.  •   The Contractor shall be solely and exclusively liable and responsible for compliance with all existing  applicable safety laws, rules and regulations, the safety of workmen, Sub - contractors, nominated  contractors, employees, including those of its   Sub - contractors or others whom they may engage  or employ for the purpose of this Contract after written permission from HIL  •   The Contractor shall ensure that its Sub - contractors/nominated Contractors do abide by the safety  rules and practices strictly inclu ding use of proper personal protective equipment by their  employee as well as his employees working at Site at their own cost.  •   The contractor shall appoint only qualified supervisors having relevant experience for deploying in  each location for effective s upervision at work.  •   The contractor must ensure the pre &   periodic   medical check - up of their workers as per the  factories Act 1948 and submit the report in the prescribed format.  •   The contractor shall ensure the use of safety appliances/Personal Protective E quipment (PPE) as  per the requirement related to the job (example   -   Rubber hand gloves for handling chemicals. Cut  resistance - 5 Hand Gloves for handling sharp edges, wire rope, MS angle, channels etc. including  rigger's work. Leather/ heat resistance Hand   Gloves for handling hot surfaces/materials including  welder's work.   Job specific g loves to be used as per the guidelines of the HIL  •   All lifting tools (Chain Pulley block, Lift, Winch, Crane, Hydra ,   slings, hooks, D -   Shackles etc.) must  be tested and certif ied by a competent person once in a year duly approved by Competent  Authorities (Director of Factories & Boilers,   of State ). A copy of the test certificate should be  submitted to Safety cell before starting the work.  •   No mobile lifting appliances shall be u sed on a sloping surface unless adequate precautions are  taken to ensure stability.  •   Daily check of Safety belts, Scaffolding, Electrical Tools etc. as per the HIL guidelines.  •   Adequate nos of Fire extinguishers near Gas cylinders, DG sets etc. First Aid   box at site office  •   Competent and qualified Safety officer(s) from day one at site as per the HIL guidelines.  •   The Contractor shall take all preventive steps to keep working place, office free from fire hazard  materials to the fullest extent. In case any fir e hazard material is kept near site or work place as a  consumable for the day's use,   adequate fire protection measures shall be   taken,   making temporary  barricade and Visuals for no smoking, no welding and gas cutting zones shall be displayed and  appropriat e fire extinguishers shall be installed (Use of cartridge type portable fire extinguisher is  not allowed in HIL) .  •   No smoking, No intake of alcohol, guthka   inside the   HIL Premises .  •   Emergency vehicle with driver during all the time when any workmen at sit e.

HEALTH & SAFETY GUIDELINES FOR CONTRACTORS  5  •   Good housekeeping must be practiced at all times while within the plant. During and after  completion of the work they shall ensure that their work area is kept clean and tidy.  •   Contractor shall ensure that he will observe all safety precautions while car rying out the job.  •   Contractor shall be solely responsible for any accident to men and machines which occur s   due to  noncompliance   of   any   safety measures. If a reportable accident occur s   due to your negligence,  action will be taken against you and penalty wi ll be imposed. If two reportable accidents   occur  during a year to a contract agency, the contract will be terminated.  •   The contractor shall have insurance policy /ESI   for his workmen under workmen compensation act  before commencement of work as per the statu tory requirement.  •   Contractor will issue gate pass for their employees in line with our HR policy for easy identification  of their employees at site of work.  •   Contractor engaged in the transportation of materials or vehicle engaged inside the   HIL Premises  c omply with all   Motor Vehicle act and Motor Vehicle Rules -   basic PPE, speed limit, roadworthiness,  insurance, PUC etc. No person to be allowed to board the running vehicle.   Helper/Cleaner is not  allowed in HIL Premises.  •   The contractor must have a first aid   box with all required medicines   (as per HIL Medicine Content  list)   covering his workmen at workplaces, at the contractor’s cost.  •   In case of   any   accident the person should be immediately sent to plant occupational health centre  with injury slip   and accident   report should be submitted to   HIL   Safety officer.  •   In case of any accident of the contractor employees, the contractor will have to take care for the  medical treatment/ compensation.  •   Contract workman engaged for Working at or construction site or in confin ed space or near high  temperature or very low temperature or work with any gas or chemicals which has effects on  respiratory or cardiac system ,   must not be suffering from uncontrolled Hypertension, Diabetes,  Cardiac pathology, Vision defect ,   hearing   defect, Asthma, Vertigo, Claustrophobia & Epilepsy.  •   Contractor working inside the plant is not allowed to store combustible material in his any of the  go - downs /storeroom. In case of storage of the same as per job requirement, prior written  permission from   execution   department ,   Safety & security   must be taken. The contractor in this  regard shall ensure compliance of legal rules & regulations.   No explosive material should be  entered in plant premises if required then approval should be taken from regulatory   authorities as  well as concern plant, security & safety department .  •   Contractors must provide the safe work arrangements for all jobs.  •   Adequate housekeeping at working site(s) to be maintained during & after closing of the job. All  materials including waste   must be put at defined end location(s).  •   PPE availability shall be submitted before gate pass & name of the person along with blood group  to be mentioned   on   the helmet.  •   Colour coding of all lifting tools and tackles   for the periodic third - party test by co mpetent authority  to be maintained as provided by client.  •   Proper drinking   water   and other welfare facili ties   to be provided by the contractor.  •   Safety of man machine and material is the essence of this safety code of conduct. In the event of  non -   compliance   of clauses of safety code by workmen engaged by the contractor HIL will  undertake specific training to workman or group of workmen so that the non - compliances of

C.   Safety Non - Compliance:  •   If the Contractor fails to take all due precautions to ensure the safety of its staff and   labor and  implementation at site, HIL will withheld 10% of its payment until such time the same gets  implemented. In case of continual noncompliance over a period, Co mpany will be forced   to  suspend the work and the cost of such suspension will be at Contractors account. The regular audit  of safety compliance at site will be carried out by HIL safety officers/FCA at site and the report in  this matter will be binding on   the Contractor .  •   Safety: The contractor shall be responsible to take all precautions to ensure safety of the labours /  workers at work. The contractor will supply his labours / worker’s safety equipment as per rules.  •   In case of any incident like Near miss / First aid/ Medical treatment injury/ LTI/ Fatal etc., must be  reported immediately verbally over telephone or in the format available with the safety dept.  •   In addition to the above, the contractor must submit the consolidated information in the format  pr escribed by the Safety Department of HIL or FCA   on monthly basis.  D.   Discipline at Work Area:  Contractor must maintain discipline at work area. He must keep the area neat and clean   during  and   after work is over.  •   Contractor must ensure the equipment such as   Crane / mobile crane, Hoists, Wire rope, Chain  slings, Chain pulley block, lifting tackles, and Forklift & Safety belt etc. are to be tested and certified  by the relevant authority and approved by the Director of Factories.  •   Wooden scaffolding is strictly   banned inside the   HIL   plant.  •   Special   risk   jobs   involving   height   job,   confined   space   work,   electrical   job,   Hot   work   at  flammable/chemical area, excavation job must be carried out with PTW. Clearance must be  obtained from a SME of concerned Task Force.  •   Cont ractor must submit the valid copy of approvals from relevant authority/Director of Factories  to the concerned Engineer In - charge   of HIL  •   Contractor must ensure that the electrical equipment’s (welding m/c, hand - drilling m/c, cutting  m/c, circuit breaker a nd grinding m/c etc.) are certified by the relevant authorities for their smooth  working.  •   Contractors must start the work after obtaining the necessary work permit from the concerned  Engineer In - charge   of HIL .  •   Contractors must ensure that the entire job carried out at site should be as per the compliance  with Safety, Health & Environment procedures.  •   Contractors must follow the control measures to ensure that the risks are eliminated / mitigated.  •   After comple tion of work, contractors shall move the scrap to the specified location of the scrap  yard .

HEALTH & SAFETY GUIDELINES FOR CONTRACTORS  7  3 .   DEFINITION AND ACRONYMS:  The following definitions apply to this document:  Authorized   –   Those persons permitted in a work area by HIL or the contractor because they are aware  of the potential hazards, the precautions to be taken and they possess the required certifications and  licenses as per regulatory requirements (e.g., electricians, tow er climbers, crane operators, aerial lift  operators).  Competent Person   –   An individual who, by way   of   training,   education,   and   experience, is  knowledgeable of applicable standards, capable of identifying workplace hazards or environmental  aspects relating   to the specific operation, is designated by the employer and has the authority to take  appropriate corrective actions.  Critical Lift   -   Any lift: utilizing multiple cranes; exceeding 85% *1 of total capacity of the crane at lift  radius; over an occupied s tructure or public street; of lifting an item of high value or long replacement  time.  Dynamic Risk Assessment   -   The continuous process of identifying hazards, assessing risk, taking action  to eliminate or reduce risk, monitoring and reviewing, in the rapi dly changing circumstances of an  operational incident.  Hot work   -   Any work involving burning, welding, torch cutting, grinding where sparks are produced,  soldering, or brazing in construction,   maintenance,   and fabrication.  High risk   -   Refers to a risk le vel that is not acceptable, after evaluating the risk of a work activity, and  must be mitigated to at least a medium level before the work begins. Risk control measures should  not be overly dependent on PPE and management review is required prior to work b eginning.  Inclement weather   -   The existence of rain, such as typhoons and rainstorms or abnormal climatic  conditions (whether they be those of hail, snow, cold, high wind, severe dust storm, lightening,  extreme high temperature or the like or any combinat ion thereof) by virtue of which it is either not  reasonable or not safe for workers exposed thereto to continue working whilst the same prevail.  Lost Time Injury (LTI)   -   An occupational injury or illness resulting in lost productive worktime. An injury  is   considered an LTI only when the employee is not   resumed his   regular work duty   with in 48 Hrs .  Medium risk   -   Refers to a risk level that is tolerable but careful evaluation of hazards associated with  the work activity must be carried out to ensure the ris k is reduced to as low as reasonably practicable.  Interim control measures like administrative controls and PPE may be used while long term control  measures are being established.  Method Statement   –   Sometimes referred to as a Safe System of Work” (SSOW),   describing the way a  work task or process is to be completed. As a minimum, details site location, landlord’s details, known  hazards on site and details of nearest hospital or medical centre. The method statement should outline  the hazards involved and inc lude a   step - by - step   guide on how to do the job safely. The method  statement must also include which control measures have been introduced to ensure   the safety of  anyone who is affected by the task or process.  Safety Data Sheets (SDSs)   –   An important compon ent of product stewardship and occupational safety  and health. It is intended to provide workers and emergency personnel with procedures for handling
--- Page 578 ---

Supplier Personnel   –   Includes, but is not limited to, supplier’s direct, full time, part - t ime,   temporary,  and contracted employees, consultants, contractors and any other third parties hired or used by the  supplier to provide the services or perform the work at the HIL assigned work sites.  4 GUIDELINES  4.1   Requirements:  •   As noted above, the requirements set forth in this   document   are the minimum expectations  of   HIL . No   HIL   entity may make representations, guarantees, or warranties that these  requirements, by themselves, represent appropriate safety practices as may be reas onable  and customary in the industry, nor shall any statements by any   HIL   employees be interpreted  as making any such representation, guarantee, or warranty.   The Contractor remains liable for  the safe performance of the work and   HIL   reserves all rights an d does not waive any claims  regarding the safe performance of the work.  •   The   contractor   shall   take   all   reasonable   actions   to   prevent   personal   injuries   and  environmental incidents associated with the work to be performed.  •   The contractor shall designate an   H&S representative who will be responsible for ensuring all  H&S requirements are followed at all work sites including monitoring,   inspection,   and  supervision of next tier contractors.   The contractor may designate one of its contractors’  employees as H&S r epresentative, but the contractor will remain directly responsible for the  H&S performance and compliance with this manual and all applicable laws and regulations.  Contractors shall hold a safety briefing (daily   toolbox   safety meeting) covering the precaut ions  to   be taken, before start of the job .  •   Safe access and egress to the work site to be   always maintained .  •   All H&S documentation related to the work to be   maintained   by contractor   and the same  must be provided to   HIL   or affiliates upon request.  4.2 T raining and Qualifications  To improve each worker’s awareness and knowledge of safe work and the prevention of accidents and  incidents, HIL requires that all persons working for and / or on behalf of the Contractor have  appropriate training. For example,   measures to protect workers from   hazard   in the workplace,  requirements   to   handle   machines   and   /or   dangerous   substances   and   have   the   appropriate  qualifications   require   to perform the work where required.  1.   The contractor shall ensure that their employees and   the employees of their subcontractors,  if any, receive appropriate training and instructions and are both competent and able to  perform the work in a healthy, safe, and environmentally acceptable manner.  2.   The   contractor   shall   provide   acceptable   proof   of   q ualifications,   safety   training   or  certifications, for all such employees to the applicable   HIL   entity upon request. For example   –  certifications / licenses as locally required for electricians, tower climbers, aerial lifts, crane  and powered industrial lift truck operators etc.
--- Page 579 ---
HEALTH & SAFETY GUIDELINES FOR CONTRACTORS  9  4.3 Emergency Response  To eliminate risks as much as possible,   HIL   re quires the Contractor to take appropriate actions and  create a plan for emergency response.  •   The contractor shall immediately respond to emergency situations involving their employees  and those activities directly under their control (including the control   of any of their  subcontractors) commensurate to the event.  •   In HIL   premises, the contractor shall become familiar with and follow the appropriate local  emergency response plan and notification procedures.  •   In the absence of a customer emergency response pl an, the contractor shall develop and  follow its own Emergency Response Plan, which shall include the appropriate on - site response  to emergencies and the notification of the appropriate external authorities and emergency  response personnel in a timely manne r.  •   The Emergency Response Plan shall address the actions to be taken by the contractor’s  employees or their subcontractors in response to fire, evacuations, accidents, injuries, work -  induced illnesses, environmental   incidents,   and any other event that cre ates or could  reasonably create   an emergency .  4.4 Notification  It is HIL’s goal to prevent all accidents and incidents in the workplace. However, in the event of such  accidents and incidents, the contractor shall, in order of priority, ensure the proper r escue and aid of  victims, notify the appropriate authorities of HIL.  •   The contractor shall make all legally required accident / incidents notifications including “Lost  Time Injury” and “Near Miss” accidents to the appropriate authorities and HIL representa tives  within the time limits prescribed by such authorities.  •   The contractor shall notify the applicable HIL representative as soon as is reasonably possible  (preferably within 12 hours) after personal injuries, fatalities, environmental incidents,  property damage or regulatory inspection so that such notification do es not, in any way, delay  the handling of the emergency or affects the response time.  •   The contractor shall work with HIL Investigation teams as required to ensure a complete  investigation including root cause analysis with appropriate corresponding correc tive and  preventive actions and report it to the HIL representative.  4.5 Risk Assessment  To identify, evaluate and decide on precautions required for the mitigation of hazards and risks in the  workplace,   HIL   requires the Contractor to conduct risk assess ments.  •   During the   Work   planning phase, the contractor shall conduct and document a generic or   Job  specific risk assessment of the operations, facilities, and equipment applicable to the work to  be performed. All work activities identified with “High” risk   must be controlled and the  elimination of such activities is to be considered. If elimination is not possible, at least  mitigation of “Medium” risk before work begins.  •   Method Statement is to be prepared and documented for each installation. It shall outl ine the  hazards involved and include a step - by - step guide on how to do the job safely. It shall also  detail which control measures have been specified to ensure the safety of anyone who is
--- Page 580 ---
HEALTH & SAFETY GUIDELINES FOR CONTRACTORS  10  affected by the task or process and be used to communicate the risk   and precautions required  to all those involved in the work. It should be clear and illustrated by simple sketches where  necessary. Equipment needed for safe working shall be clearly identified and available before  work starts. Workers should know what to   do if the work method needs to be changed.  Emergency arrangements shall be included in the method statement.  •   On arrival at the   Job   site, and before any work is undertaken, the contractor shall conduct an  on - site risk assessment, often referred to as a “dy namic” risk assessment, evaluating site -  specific hazards including the ones affected by inclement weather, how they could affect the  Job   and the means of mitigation. This “dynamic” risk assessment shall be recorded and signed  off   by   all   persons   involved   in   the   Job .   Sign - off   shall   demonstrate   agreement   and  understanding of the “dynamic” risk assessment. When visitors arrive on site during t he  works, an H&S induction to be completed. The “dynamic” risk assessment to be used as part  of this induction process.  •   The contractor shall promptly notify the applicable   HIL   representative of any defect, unsafe,  unhealthy,   or environmentally unsound con dition identified during the “dynamic” risk  assessment that prevents or may prevent a safe and proper installation from being completed.  When a risk or hazard cannot be mitigated, work is to be stopped until it is safe to proceed  4.6 Site Inspections / Cor rective Actions / Post Job Evaluations  HIL requires the Contractor to take corrective actions on observed hazards or potentially hazardous  conditions when such hazards are identified through site inspections.  •   The contractor shall conduct and document peri odic site inspections to identify and correct  any observed or potentially unsafe or environmentally unacceptable conditions.  •   Documented   periodic   site   inspections   to   be   made   available   to   the   applicable   HIL  representative or any of their representatives upo n request. The contractor shall promptly  correct any observed or potentially hazardous conditions at the contractor’s expense if the  condition is of their making and notify HIL if the condition was not caused by the contractor.  •   The contractor shall collab orate with and contribute to post Job Evaluations as required.  4.7 Work in Inclement Weather  Serious and fatal accidents may occur at workplaces in inclement weather conditions, such as the loss  of body balance resulting in falls, electric   shock,   or elect rocution due to leakage of current from wet  electrical equipment and the slipping of tools or equipment. The contractor should not underestimate  the danger of inclement weather and should adopt a flexible approach and   consider   employees’ safety  both in th e workplace and during their journeys to and from work.   Before start the job contractor’s  supervisor will check the weather condition and mention in daily tool box register and also describe  with his working crew .   HIL requires the Contractor to   –  •   Stop wor k at places with risks of falling objects or fall of persons and avoid operating cranes  S uch as tower cranes , Hydra   etc.  •   R emove or secure loose materials.  •   S ecure plant and scaffolds.  •   S tay inside a safe shelter away from metal pipes, cable, structure, fe nces or window glasses.  •   N ot perform work at height that cannot be safely performed.  •   N ot use an umbrella which can cause loss of body balance due to strong wind.

HEALTH & SAFETY GUIDELINES FOR CONTRACTORS  11  •   U se suitable PPE e.g. safety helmets.  •   U se safe means of transport and have a route for evac uation.  •   A lways remain alert on changes to the working environment.  •   P repare for evacuation at any moment.  •   B e familiar with the escape route in case of emergency.  •   F ollow established   HIL   safety procedures.   No use of Mobile Phone.  •   R esume work only when conditions improve.  During a lightning thunderstorm the contractors shall  •   K eep away from antennas, masts, guy wires and all grounding and lightning protection  equipment, including ground rods   and don’t take shelter under trees .  •   N ot   work on conductive materials such as electrical lines, tower structures or pipelines and  not wear highly conductive equipment, e.g. a   headset.  •   I f in a vehicle, stay in the vehicle until the lightning ends. Avoid touching metal or other  surfaces in or outs ide the vehicle that conduct electricity.  •   Not lie down on a concrete floor or lean against a wall.  4.8 Chemical Management  Chemical substances have the potential risk to cause considerable health and environmental problems  if they are not appropriately   managed. To eliminate these problems and hazards, HIL requires the  Contractor to manage and handle such substances in a proper manner. ·  •   All chemicals used in the course of the work shall be included in the risk assessment.  •   T he contractor is responsible f or ensuring that all users are properly trained in the safe use  and disposal of any hazardous material or chemical involved in or related to the project.  •   Chemicals and other hazardous materials must be properly labelled with the name of the  chemical or ma terial and its environmental, safety, and / or health hazards.  •   Appropriate PPE shall be used when handling chemicals   as prescribed in MSDS .  •   Chemicals   and   other   hazardous   materials   shall   be   properly   stored   according   to   the  manufacturer’s recommendations a nd any applicable laws or regulations.  •   Except for materials intended to be left as part of a project deliverable, chemicals and other  hazardous materials shall be removed from   HIL   premises upon completion of the work  activities.  •   M SDS’s shall be readily a vailable to the   HIL   and the individuals working on the site for any  hazardous materials and chemicals as   applicable and   provided upon request.  •   Secondary containment facility should available near the chemical storage area.  •   All chemicals should store accor ding to compatibility chart  4.9 Barricades, Signs & Perimeter Guarding  When the work is performed, taking precautions   to prevent any incident   is mandatory. HIL requires  the Contractor to   display   visible warning signs so that all workers and the public are aware of the  dangers in the workplace.  •   Signs and temporary guarding, such as cones, tape, and posting guards, shall be used to warn  individuals of potentially hazardous situations caused by or   relating to the work being
--- Page 582 ---
HEALTH & SAFETY GUIDELINES FOR CONTRACTORS  12  performed. Such work shall include, but not be limited to, excavation, trenching, electrical,  construction, tower erection or climbing, antenna installation, and cable work. All barricades  shall be appropriate for the application , visible, legible, and able to withstand any adverse  conditions present at the work site and comply with all applicable laws and regulations.  •   If the work restricts traffic on a roadway, warnings such as signs, cones, flags, sign / traffic  worker(s), traf fic lights, or other means of communication as appropriate and in accordance  with local requirements shall be provided. Proper precautions shall be taken to communicate  the restrictions and re - direct the traffic accordingly. The initial warning sign shall   be located  and maintained at least 300 meters ahead of the work site  •   If the work is being conducted at a tower or other elevated structure, signs and temporary  guarding shall be placed at the perimeter of the work area. This is defined as the “drop zone”  a nd it shall be a minimum of 1/2 of the working height.   If a minimum of 1/2 cannot be  achieved, a third man (spotter) shall be deployed at the edge of the “drop zone” whilst  persons are working at height.  •   Personnel, having to work in an area where guards,   warning signs and / or controlled access  are being established by another contractor, shall be aware of the hazard, take necessary  precautions and follow all necessary steps to get access to and work on the subject site if  granted permission and if safe t o do so  4.10 Confined Space Entry & Rescue  A confined space can be more hazardous than regular workspaces as it is an enclosed or partially  enclosed space that is not primarily designed or intended for human occupancy with several hazards,  such as poor air   quality, chemical exposures, fire hazard and temperature extremes.  HIL requires the Contractor to identify the hazards and create a rescue program.  •   The contractor shall establish and maintain a Confined Space Entry & Rescue Program to enter  confined spa ces.  •   All confined spaces encountered during the course of the work shall be evaluated for hazards  before entry is allowed. Classification of the space shall be documented (e.g., entry permit  required) and safe work practices applicable to the type of conf ined space shall be enforced.  •   Atmospheric monitoring shall be performed to detect hazardous atmospheric conditions in  confined spaces (such as manholes and vaults) prior to entry and such monitoring shall be  documented and be made available upon request.   These spaces shall be properly ventilated  prior to and during entry in accordance with applicable H&S laws and regulations. One person  shall stand guard, when required by local laws and regulations outside the confined space  whenever another person enters   it and shall be appropriately trained and ready to implement  emergency procedures without putting themselves at risk in order to help that person if  necessary. During a confined space entry, a mechanical means to   rescue   a worker from the  space must be available and in use in the event an emergency situation arises.  •   The person(s) entering the confined space shall be provided with a means of communicating  to person(s) outside the confined space. A mobile phone or walkie - t alkie is recommended.  •   No entry shall be allowed into a confined space that has an atmosphere that is immediately  dangerous to life or health.  •   Entry and exit register should maintain during working in confined space

Non - conductive hard hats shall be utilized  when performing electrical work.

HIL  requires the Contractor to manage the risks associated with its use by identifying machinery and  equipment hazards in the workplace and eliminating or reducing the risk of those hazards than can  cause harm.  •   The contractor shall instruct and   train its employees in the safe operating procedures and  hazards of all mechanical and electrical equipment. Training shall be documented and  conducted in accordance with applicable H&S laws and regulations of HIL.  •   The contractor shall provide evidence of   the certification, either academic or issued by  government or duly recognized institution as related to its Powered Industrial Lift Truck,  forklift, and Mobile Elevated Work Platform (MEWP) or any other mechanical and electrical  equipment.  •   All mechanical   and electrical equipment shall be operated and maintained according to the  manufacturer’s recommendations.  •   All mechanical and electrical equipment shall have a calibration / inspection   test date &   due  date which is visible to the user. Documentation shal l be available that demonstrates that  mechanical and electrical equipment has been calibrated / inspected   through competent  person . All mechanical and electrical equipment shall be inspected before each use. The  equipment shall not be used unless it is fou nd to be free of defects that would affect its safe  use.  4.14 Rigging Operations  Improper rigging of a load or a rigging failure can expose riggers and other workers nearby to a variety  of potential hazards, including riggers’ being injured or killed whe n loads have slipped from the rigging,  or the rigging has failed. HIL requires the Contractor for the measures to eliminate these dangers.  •   Rigging equipment shall be adequate for the type of lift.  •   Manufacturer’s operating manuals shall be kept at the wor ksite for cranes, hoists and gin  poles.  •   Rigging equipment shall have a unique identification number that is traceable to the  manufacturer and an inspection due date, both of which are traceable via a lifting register.  Documentation shall be available that   demonstrates that rigging equipment has been  inspected.   Rigging   equipment   shall   be   inspected   at   a   frequency   stipulated   by   the  manufacturer (more frequently if specified locally) and prior to each use.  •   Employees shall be trained in the proper use of   rigging equipment.

HEALTH & SAFETY GUIDELINES FOR CONTRACTORS  16  4.15 Crane /Hydra   Operation  Cranes need particular attention paid to their operation, HIL requires the Contractor to properly plan  and operate cranes.  •   Crane operators shall be trained and also licensed / certified   and medically fit   as   per   HIL  requirements. Documentation of training and licensing/certification shall be made available.  •   The crane operator shall inspect the crane, rigging and the area of the lift before commencing  operation. Both daily and all other applicable frequencies   of crane inspection documentation  shall be made available.  •   The crane operator shall identify the work zone by marking boundaries (such as with flags or  a device such as a range limit device or range control warning device) and not operate the  equipment p ast those boundaries.  •   Equipment operations within minimum clearance distances of a power line are strictly  prohibited except where the mobile crane supplier demonstrates that all country, state and  local regulations are met.  •   A pre - lift meeting shall be held so that all parties involved in the lifting operation, including  crane operator(s), riggers, signal persons, employees on personnel lift platforms and  supervisors have a thorough understanding of how communication will take   place.  •   A single person should be designated to direct all operations during the lift. Signal persons  shall be   trained,   and documentation of that training shall be made available upon request.  •   A lifting plan shall be prepared and made available for all l ifts. The lifting plan shall include  items necessary to address the special conditions of each lift. These items shall include but  not be limited to, the total load weight to be lifted, crane placement location, required crane  configuration, sling and rigg ing selection, and diagrams of lifting area dimensions.  •   Personnel Lifting Systems utilizing either suspended or boom - attached personnel platforms  shall comply   with all the legal requirements .  •   A trial lift with the unoccupied personnel lift platform loade d at least to the anticipated lift  weight shall be made from ground level, or any other location where employees will enter the  platform, to each location at which the platform is to be hoisted and positioned.  •   Both daily and all other applicable frequenci es of inspections of personnel lift platforms shall  be made available upon request.  •   Prepare and document a Personnel Lift Plan as required by country,   state,   and local  requirements.  •   The crane’s load chart shall be available. The load chart specifies the   rated (maximum)  capacity of that machine for every permissible configuration as well as the machine’s  operational limitations and conditions necessary for safe operation.  •   Loads are never to be hoisted or moved over workers within the crane’s swing radius.  •   All personnel shall be kept clear of loads about to be lifted and of suspended loads.  •   The area within the swing radius of the crane’s superstructure shall be barricaded.  •   New Generation hydra (K - 10 series) should be used in HIL premises.  •   Mobile phone sh ould be prohibited during working with crane and hydra.  •   R e g u l ar to o l box   t a l k s   a n d   on the   j ob   tra i n i n g   o n   sa f e   material handling related training   to   b e  co n d u c t ed   b y   t he   s u p er v i s or   f or   wo r k   cr ew   b e f ore   s tart i ng   of   t h e   j o b .

HEALTH & SAFETY GUIDELINES FOR CONTRACTORS  17  4.16 Excavation and   Trenching  Trenching and excavation work presents serious hazards to all workers involved. Cave - ins pose the  greatest risk and are more likely than some other excavation - related incidents to result in worker  fatalities.   HIL   requires the Contractor to have a dequate protections in place by identifying excavation  and trenching hazards.  •   The contractor shall identify any potential hazards, including, but not limited to, the location  of existing underground utilities, and obtain all required approvals before begi nning any  excavation, trenching, or other digging operations.  •   The contactor should be taken General & equipment clearance work permit and  excavation work permit   f r o m   the   c o n c er n ed   a ut h ori t y   b e f ore   e x e c ut i on   of   the   j o b .  •   T he trench or excavation must be shored, sloped, braced, or otherwise supported to prevent  cave - ins, unless the excavation is in stable rock.  •   Ladders must be placed no more than 8 meters apar t , for immediate egress.  •   Tools, machines, or excavated material must be placed no closer than 60 centimetres from  the edge of a trench excavation and 1.2m from any other   excavation.  •   Adequate ventilation must be ensured when operating equipment with internal combustion  engines.  •   For   mechanical trenching, specific documented safety controls shall be developed and  implemented to protect the ground employees working in close proximity to the Chain or Rock  Wheel of the Trenching Equipment.  •   Workers must understand and have knowledge of t he locations of underground utilities.  •   A clear communication between the Supervisor and Drilling Operators shall be established to  avoid impacting existing utilities infrastructure.  •   The crew shall adopt an electromagnetic tracking device to avoid disrupt ing existing utilities.  •   Additionally, particular attention to pipe coupling and decoupling shall be implemented.  •   Compressed air is frequently used when dropping cable inside the piping.   HIL   Safety  procedures on working with compressed air shall be docum ented and adopted by the  contractors’ employees performing this task.  •   Appropriate warning signs, barricades, and other perimeter guarding shall be used to prevent  workers and members of the public from falling into the excavation.  •   If, during the excavati on, unknown materials are unearthed, the contractor must stop the  work immediately, notify their H&S representative, determine the nature of the materials,  and take the appropriate measures to protect human health and the environment before  proceeding. Mea sures taken must be in accordance with applicable H&S laws and regulations  of HIL .  4.17   Concreting  Shuttering and supporting structures shall be of adequate strength and approved by Engineer - in -  Charge. This shall be ensured before concrete is poured. The proc edure approved by Engineer - in -  Charge shall be followed for mixing,   transporting,   and pouring of concrete.   You have to ensure  safe operation and maintenance of Batch mixing equipment’s. The SOP for operation of Batch  mixing equipment’s approved by HIL has t o be displayed at work site.

HEALTH & SAFETY GUIDELINES FOR CONTRACTORS  18  4.18   Demolition  Before any demolition work is commenced and also during the progress of the work:  •   All roads and open area adjacent to the work site shall either be closed or suitably protected.  Appropriate warning signs shall b e displayed for cautioning approaching persons.  •   Before demolition operations begin, the Contractor shall ensure that the power on all electric  service lines /Cables   is shut off and the lines cut or disconnected at or outside the demolition  site. If it is n ecessary to maintain electric power during demolition operation, the required  service   lines   shall   be   adequately   protected   against damage.   Persons   handling   heavy  materials/ equipment’s   shall wear safety shoes.  •   No floor, roof or other part of the building shall be overloaded with debris or materials as to  render it unsafe.  •   Entries to the demolition area shall be restricted to authorized persons only  4.19 Reinforcement  •   Bar bending and cutting yard shall be prope rly cordoned / barricaded and entry shall be  restricted.  •   Re‐bar bending and cutting machines shall be handled by trained operator / skilled workers.  •   Shifting of cut re‐bars shall be done by mechanical means as far as possible. When re‐ bars are  shifted m anually, it shall be done with proper care and proper balance shall be maintained.  Clear access shall be provided for shifting of re‐bars.  •   Proper support shall be given to the column bars by means of rings / props against undesirable  sway.  •   Free ends of   the binding wires shall be bent inside to avoid injuries.  •   Proper PPE viz. leather / cotton hand gloves, goggles, etc., for the people handling / shifting  and cutting / tying of re‐bar, shall be used.  4.20 Formwork for concreting  •   Shuttering and supportin g members viz. props, tie rods, etc. shall be of adequate strength to  support the load / pressure of concrete and the formwork scheme shall be approved by  Engineer‐In‐Charge in advance. The procedure approved by Engineer‐In‐ Charge shall be  followed for mi xing, transporting and pouring of concrete.  •   The process of stripping of formwork shall be planned in advance and approved by Engineer‐  in‐charge. Stripping shall enable the structural member to behave in desired manner. The area  shall be suitably cordoned   / barricaded and unauthorized entry shall be restricted by  displaying signboards, etc.  •   While removing formwork from vertical surfaces, the shuttering board shall be adequately  supported by props, in order to prevent the same from toppling / slipping, unti l it is lowered  on ground safely. Same support with props shall be provided during erection of formwork too  until the plywood is secured in desired place with tie rods  4. 21   Ladder Safety  When using ladders, the following practices shall be followed:  •   All ladders shall be inspected and shall be in good condition prior to each use.   There should  be anti skid rubber shoe with leg of the ladder
--- Page 589 ---
HEALTH & SAFETY GUIDELINES FOR CONTRACTORS  19  •   Ladders must be made of nonconductive material (i.e., wood, or fiberglass with a plastic top)  if being used where the person on the ladder or the ladder could come in contact with  energized equipment parts/components, fixtures or circuit conductors.  •   Stepl adders shall be used only in the fully opened, locked position.  •   All ladder feet shall be positioned on secure, even surfaces; and   All employees that use ladders  shall be instructed in the proper use of ladders.  •   Use of personal fall protection equipment w hile use of ladder  •   Ladder should be use in 1:4 ration.  •   During working on ladder it should be tied with permanent structure and one crew member  also hold the ladder.  4. 2 2   Work at Height  The Contractor shall engage only properly trained and certified climb ers who are fully aware of and  knowledgeable about the inherent dangers of climbing or working above ground level. The climber’s  training and knowledge shall include:  •   The nature of fall hazards in the work area.  •   The correct procedures for erecting, maint aining, disassembling, and inspecting the fall  protection systems to be used.  •   The correct procedures for inspecting personal fall protection equipment for wear, damage,  defect or deterioration.  •   Climbing safety procedures  •   The use and operation of the fal l protection systems utilized by the contractor/sub - contractor.  •   The role of each employee in the safety monitoring system used  •   The correct procedures for handling and storing equipment and materials  •   The compatibility of fall protection equipment and fall   protection systems  •   Hands - on field exercise that will cover climbing, repositioning, and rescue on the type of  elevated structures tower climbers will encounter in the field. HEALTH & SAFETY GUIDELINES FOR CONTRACTORS  22  4.2 5   Road Safety  •   All vehicles shall have valid registration; insurance & road permit in compliance with  regulations & always keep copies of valid travelling documents in the   vehicle (Driving  license, registration, insurance, identity card & contact details).  •   All vehicles shall be equipped with seat belts, first aid box, handy fire extinguisher,  standard stopper, etc. The drivers should be trained to use fire extinguishers.  •   All   vehicles shall be well maintained & kept in perfect working order and fully equipped  with the proper safety gear. Conduct regular checks of the vehicle’s condition and report  defects immediately. Any defect is removed as soon as practicable, before the ve hicle is  put in use.  •   Pick - ups and similar vehicles shall be equipped with cargo barriers to sufficiently stop  projectiles and sharp objects from striking the driver and the passengers.  •   Comply with HIL defined Health & Safety procedures for the contract veh icles & drivers  as per Contract Management system.  •   All drivers shall have an experience of minimum 5 years and age should be between 23 &  58 years.  •   All contractors shall wear   ISI Mark   crash helmet and shoes while driving motorbike. Safety  helmets worn in p lants will not qualify as crash helmets, as they are not adequate to  withstand the impact caused during accident.  •   Any new driver before starting driving shall attend safety training program for safe driving.  •   Drivers shall have his journey schedule showing   expected date & time to complete the  journey.  •   Drivers shall not drive more than 08 hrs. in a day.  •   Drivers shall operate only those vehicles for which they are trained, authorized, and  licensed.  •   Without proper authorization by their respective supervisors,   drivers shall not operate  any vehicle other than they are authorized to operate, even if they are capable of such  operation.  •   The person in the driver's seat shall keep seat belts fastened, while the vehicle is in  motion.  •   Specified and safe speed limits sh all be observed and always maintained.  •   Specified vehicle operating instructions shall be observed.  •   Driving during bad weather conditions shall be done with utmost care.  •   Mobile phones are not to be used whilst driving or operating a vehicle.  •   Driving under t he influence of alcohol or any sedative drug (including prescribed  medication) is strictly prohibited.  •   Eating, drinking (even non - alcoholic beverages), etc. during driving shall be avoided. Such  activities increase the risk of accident due to distraction a nd lack of concentration.  •   When   loading   and   unloading,   the   driver   shall   observe   relevant   guidelines   and  requirements in a way to avoid danger to any person or damage to any property.
--- Page 593 ---
HEALTH & SAFETY GUIDELINES FOR CONTRACTORS  23  •   Drivers shall have their medical fitness report before resuming their job   at site as per  regulation; and once a year thereafter & copy of medical report shall be available with the  driver.  •   Should be trained in First Aid and CPR and Transport safety before start of work and  should possess a valid certificate.  •   Should   undergo refresher training at least once a year at the cost of the contractor.  •   Violation of the guidelines may result to accidents. In any case   HIL   reserves the right of  taking appropriate action (including termination of services or contracts) against vio lators  of the guidelines.  •   In case of an ACCIDENT, Near Miss and Major Vehicle break down, Driver / Travel person  shall Immediately report the incident to the concerned responsible person, follow the  defined procedures of Indian Motor Vehicle.  •   TREM Card mus t be available with the driver during transportation and Haz - Chem code  must be displayed on the chemical transportation vehicles.  •   All the vehicles should be parked in designated parking area and should follow the  designated route while going to coming from   their respective work site.  •   Should be well versed in vehicle safety/ transport safety and chemical safety checklists as  per the work assigned.  4.2 6   Material Handling  •   A suitable lifting safety program/ safe system of work is developed consisting of plann ing and  execution of lifting operations.  •   Risk assessments and permit to work are required for all lifting operation.  •   The worksite is physically safe for the lifting operation e.g., there is no physical obstruction in the  path of the load.  •   The lifting equip ment is correctly selected, tested and certified in accordance with legislative  requirements and technical standards.  •   The personnel deployed as part of the lifting team are suitably trained, competent, and certified.  •   All lifting equipment and accessories a re tested prior to commissioning and periodically by  competent persons for rated capacity and records are   maintained.  •   Lifting devices and equipment have been certified for use within the last 12 months (at a  minimum);  •   Load does not exceed dynamic and/or st atic capacities of the lifting equipment.  •   Any safety devices installed on lifting equipment are operational; and  •   All lifting devices and equipment have been visually examined before each lift by competent  person.  •   Every lifting equipment and its component parts shall be identified with a visible marking including  the  •   Safe Working Load (SWL).  •   Date of Last Test  •   Next due date
--- Page 594 ---
HEALTH & SAFETY GUIDELINES FOR CONTRACTORS  24  •   While crane marching & placement experienced flagman shall navigate the crane movements and  provide the directions to crane operator while marching on road.  •   Wherever needed soil compaction shall be undertaken and steel plates shall be used to provide a  firm base to lifting equipment/crane.  •   Barricading/ warnings signs shall be provided to   prevent moving below suspended load or the  boom areas of potential impact.  •   Cranes shall not be operated between sunset and sunrise without adequate lighting provision.  •   Test Certificates: All current test certificates for cranes and lifting gear shall be ke pt readily  available. Lifting equipment and accessories shall not be used unless the appropriate current test  certificates are available.  Any Deviations must be informed to concerned authority and proper approval shall be taken before  proceeding with restr icted deviations. All working crew must be aware of the specific deviations  including risks & hazards which may occur during execution with deviations. Deviations must be  documented, and documentation must indicate causes of deviation with safety plan. Dev iation  authorization must be renewed periodically.  4.2 7   Fire Protection  The Contractor shall take all preventive steps to keep working place, office free from fire hazard  materials to the fullest extent.   Packing materials like polythene, cardboard, wood   etc. shall be  immediately removed after unpacking to the scrap storage areas earmarked by the Company.   Every  day, any combustible debris, wastage and other inflammable articles shall be removed to dumping  area. Flammable gas, chemicals, fuels; oils shal l be stored away from working site, office and living  places and precautions taken to keep in fire preventive/proof area where smoking, naked fire, loose  electrical connection, sparking etc. should be avoided. In case any fire hazard material is kept near  site or work place as a consumable for the day's   use, adequate fire protection measures, making  temporary barricade and marking   no smoking, no welding and gas cutting zones shall be carried out  and displayed and appropriate fire extinguishers installe d. Fire extinguisher at different places of  work, offices, living places shall be installed at adequate nos. and type suitable for fire of supervisors,  engineers and workmen should be trained to use fire extinguishers and fire hydrants so that every  time a nd every place trained persons are available to operate appliances and fire hydrants.   Besides  telephone nos. of the Company's fire brigade as well as nearest fire brigades of the neighbouring  organizations and Govt. shall be displayed at work site, offic e, living places.   All fire arrangements as  stated above, adequacy certified by the Company's Fire Officer or appropriate Fire authority shall be  done by the Contractor at appropriate time at his cost and periodical check shall be carried out to  ensure th at they are in order in addition to installation of the Contractor's own  fire fighting arrangement including Fire Tender, if required.   The Fire Tender operator shall have  undergone training course in statutory organization of Govt./approved by Govt. and c ompetency  certificate in this respect shall be furnished to the Engineer  4.2 8   Painting  •   The following precautions shall be taken during the Painting work.  •   Air respirators shall be provided for use by the workers when paint is applied in the form of  spray, or a surface having lead paint is dry rubbed or scraped.  •   A dequate facilities shall be provided to enable the painters to wash at the cessation of work.
--- Page 595 ---
HEALTH & SAFETY GUIDELINES FOR CONTRACTORS  25  •   A ll painting jobs, especially those in which lead paints are used shall be kept under industrial  hygiene surveillance.  •   Smoking, open flames or sources of ignition shall not be allowed in places where paints and other  flammable substances are stored, mixed o r used. A caution board, with the instructions written  in national/regional language, “SMOKING IS STRICTLY PROHIBITED” shall be displayed in the  vicinity where painting is in progress or where paints are stored. Symbols shall also be used for  caution board s.  •   Suitable fire   extinguishers shall   be kept available at places where flammable paints are stored,  handled or used.  •   When painting work is done in a closed room or in a confirmed space, adequate ventilation shall  be provided. If adequate ventilation cannot be provided, workers shall wear suitable respirators.  •   Epoxy resins and their formulations used for painting shall n ot be allowed to come in contact  with the skin. The workers shall use plastic gloves and/or suitable barrier creams.  •   Adequate ventilation shall be provided especially when working with hot resin mixes.  •   Increased personal hygiene shall be practiced to cont rol inadvertent contract with the resin and  eliminate its effects.  •   Workers shall thoroughly wash hands and feet before leaving the work. Work clothes be changed  and laundered frequently.  5.   OCCUPATIONAL   HEALTH,   SAFETY & ENVIRONMENT O (OHS&E):  The contrac tor shall be responsible to take all precautions to ensure safety of the labours / workers at  work. The contractor will supply his labours / worker’s safety equipment/PPEs as per rules. If you're  bringing your own equipment to carryout of job inside the pl ant such equipment should be subjected  to hazard identifications and risk assessment prior to commencing of work. The persons engaged by  you shall be given appropriate awareness on OH&S. Those personnel who will carry out jobs affecting  OH&S shall be prope rly trained and made competent for the job to be performed by them. During  emergency which may be faced in the plant your personnel should move to the designated emergency  assembly points. They should not spread any rumour.  •   It is preferable to have Occupa tional health and safety standard implemented by contractor at  his site.  •   Contractor shall follow all Safety, Health, Environment, and other operational policies as  published by Hindalco time to time with latest amendments.  •   Contractor shall follow all   Safety, Health, Environment, and other operational policies.  •   Suppression of Fugitive dust emissions due to vehicle movement of contractor’s vehicle will be  responsibility of the contractor.  •   Contractors shall have to avoid spillage for all kind of materials . In case any spillage happens, it  must be cleaned by the contractor at his own cost.  •   Ash must be transported in covered condition.  •   Contractors shall have to follow the respective control measures while carrying out a particular  activity to ensure complian ce with environmental standards.
--- Page 596 ---
HEALTH & SAFETY GUIDELINES FOR CONTRACTORS  26  •   Contractors need to ensure Pollution under Control (PUC) Certificate and fitness certificate  obtained for the vehicle used for transportation. Drivers have to show these certificates as and  when required along with their Dr iving License.  •   Contractors shall have to ensure that there are no air horns fitted to their vehicles and the noise  from vehicles do not increase the overall ambient noise levels.  •   Contractors shall educate their vehicle drivers regarding the use of correct   fuel and to explain  them ill effects of using adulterated fuel.  •   Contractors should avoid over loading of trucks.  •   Contractors and their employees should follow SOP for handling of all types of wastes.  •   Contractors and their employees should not wash or carry   out any maintenance to the vehicle  inside the Plant.  •   Contractors must ensure that none of the resources (water, energy, steam, compressed air, etc.)  are misused.  •   Contractors must ensure good housekeeping in their workplace.  •   Contractors / transporters must   ensure that trucks carrying hazardous chemical like Caustic Soda  Lye, Sulphuric Acid, Hydrochloric Acid etc has to display the Transport Emergency Card (TREM  card) and their drivers should be trained to handle the material during emergency  •   Transporters ca rrying hazardous waste should have necessary authorization from SPCB and  should carry manifest while carrying waste from   HIL .  Contractors must ensure that none of their work will result in pollution of air, water, soil & noise  6. SAFETY OFFICER:  •   The   contractor shall have competent & dedicated safety officer / supervisor to ensure Compliance  to safety measures / rules and regulations. The guidelines for deciding number of safety officer /  supervisor:  •   If average manpower deployment on daily basis per contractor for the specific contract falls in  between   1   –   10 0 persons, one Safety Officer   will be required. If deployment exceeds 50 nos.  additional Safety officers will be required in the scale of 1 safety   officer for every 50 men deployed.  •   Irrespective of the no. of manpower deployment, dedicated Safety Officer will be required per shift  for the following job contract (at each location):  •   De - scaling/ working in confined space.  •   Working at height  •   Lifting &   handling of heavy items  •   Scaffolding & similar activities  •   Major electrical jobs like Switchgear maintenance  •   Work with chemicals / gases  •   Any construction job.  •   Loading, unloading job.  •   Heavy vehicle use  •   Persons involved in transport whether road or railways.  •   Minimum qualification for the Safety Officer/Supervisor will be Engineering (Mech./Elect/Civil) or  Diploma in Engineering (Mech./Elect/Civil) and Graduate in Science with Post Diploma in industrial  Safety from a recognized institute approved by SCVT/AICT E. The assessment of competency and  verification of employability under contractor will be done at site.

Hindalco Industries Limited  Electrical Safety Standard   Document No.   HI L/ O HS/STD / ES/01  Effective Date   19.03.2020  Review Date   19.03.2022  Page   4   of 58  1.   General  Electricity is as dangerous as it is useful. Unlike other products we use every day, we can't see,   hear  or   smell electricity. So we may not always think about using it safely. A moment of carelessness, or  a piece of   faulty equipment, can cause an electrical accident. Minor electrical shocks could be fatal  and minor errors   while handling electrical equip ment could hurt you. The more we learn about the  safe use of electricity, the   less our chance of injury. Electricity gives us light but do not take electricity  lightly!   Power systems often have one side of the voltage supply connected to earth ground to en sure  safety at   that side, by keeping zero potential in between. The "grounded" conductor is called the  neutral conductor,   while the ungrounded conductor is called the phase or the live wire. Electric shock  can only occur when   contact is made between two po ints of a circuit; when voltage is applied across  a victim's body. Electric   burns, fires, arcing and explosion are other versions of electrical accidents.  Effective implementation of   Electrical Safety precautions can save lives and to prevent hazards.  2.   Purp o s e  Working on or near energized electrical installations/ equipment’s   can   cause se rious risks if  installations if   not   adequately engineered, maintained and operated. Exposure to live electrical  installations / cable is major source of electrical injuries besides exposure to electrical arc flash and  fire due to electrical short circuit. The purpose of this standard is to   provide adequate guidance to  work   with   minimum   safety   requirements   &   precautions   to   be   taken   during   commissioning   /  installations and provide information and knowledge to the working group so that they can develop  procedure for safe operation and maintenan ce of electrical installations / equipment’s.  3.   Scope  The purpose of this standard is to provide a   practical safe working area for employees relative to  the   hazards arising from the use of electricity.  4.   Reference     Indian Electricity Rules 1956     Indian Electrici ty Act   1910     Electricity Act 2003     CE A   Regulation 2010
--- Page 603 ---
Hindalco Industries Limited  Electrical Safety Standard   Document No.   HI L/ O HS/STD / ES/01  Effective Date   19.03.2020  Review Date   19.03.2022  Page   5   of 58     Battery Management & handling Rules - 2001     E - waste (Management) Rules - 2016     OSHA, Electrical Safety Related Work Practices, 29 CFR Part 1910     NFPA® 70E, "Electrical Safety Requirements for Employee   Workplaces"     Re quirement under Hindalco -   Electrical Task Force   Safety committee     Safety Policy: ABG/SUST/POL/06     Machine Guarding: ABG/SUST/TS/33     Electrical Safety: ABG/SUST/GN/31     Personal Protective Equipment: ABG/SUST/GN/32     Portable Hand and Power Tools: ABG/SUST/GN/35     Documented Information Management: ABG/SUST/MS/06.     SAFETY MANAGEMENT SYSTEM : ABG/SUST/TS/14     E LECTRICAL SAFETY : ABG/SUST/GN/31     PERSONAL PROTECTIVE EQUIPMENT : ABG/SUST/GN/32  5.   Definition  These definitions   are provided to describe the intent of this standard; the terms may be defined  differently in other contexts.  Electrical Safety Officer :   A   person being determined by   management,   he possesses a certificate  of competency o n electrical work   issued   by the Appropriate Government   for ensuring observance  of safety measures specified under   CE A   Regulation 2010.  Designating & Authorized Person   — a   person being determined by management he possesses  a certificate of competency o n electrical work   issued   by the Appropriate   /Competent authority  / Government   and given permission and support to perform and/or direct specific tasks or  functions.  Qualified Person :  1.   Trained and knowledgeable in the construction and operation of equipment or specific work  method.  2.   Able to recognize and avoid electrical hazards .  3.   May be qualified with respect to certain equipment and methods but unqualified for others.
--- Page 604 ---
Hindalco Industries Limited  Electrical Safety Standard   Document No.   HI L/ O HS/STD / ES/01  Effective Date   19.03.2020  Review Date   19.03.2022  Page   6   of 58  4.   Mus t be authorized by unit to work &   only   qualified persons shall be allowed to work on  energized parts or equipment .  Electrical Work:   Any job or task requiring a Qualified Electrical Worker. It includes any work  that involves a shock or arc flash hazard or creates potential a shock or arc flash   haz ards,  energized or   DE energized   c ondition.  Electrical event — an unusual occurrence that did not, but had the potential to, lead to an  electrical incident.  Electrical incident — an incident that involves, or has the potential to cause, damage to  equipment   or property or an electrical injury as defined in this standard or other injury that may  result from direct exposure to electrical energy (e.g., a fall injury that might result from reaction  to electric shock).  Electrical injury — an injury that results fro m electrical, thermal, acoustic, or radiation energy  released at the moment of an electrical incident.  Electrical Resource   -   A person who is familiar with site safety rules concerning electrical work  and understands the hazards, operation and design of the   equipment involved. This person need  not work in the same area as the involved equipment is located.  Energy - Isolating Device : A mechanical device that prevents the transmission or release  of energy. Examples include the circuit breakers and loa d dis - connector isolator switches,  Energy -   isolating devices should be lockable by some means or other type of attachment.  Energized Work   -   Work where exposed and unguarded circuits or parts energized at potentials  greater than 50 volts must be contacted o r approached closer than the "Prohibited Approach  Boundary"  Flash Rating : Maximum energy (from an arc flash) for which the PPE will provide satisfactory  protection.  High Voltage (Voltage Level) : The term high voltage applies to electrical equipme nt that  op erates at more than 65 0 Volts (for Phase to Phase operation) or more than 300 Volts (for  Phase to ground operation)
--- Page 605 ---
Hindalco Industries Limited  Electrical Safety Standard   Document No.   HI L/ O HS/STD / ES/01  Effective Date   19.03.2020  Review Date   19.03.2022  Page   7   of 58  Insulation Resistance .   The insulation resistance measurement test may be   conducted on all  types of electrical switchgear, transformers, cables, etc. The test is a method of determining the  integrity or condition of equipment insulation.  Electrical Grounding (Earthing ):   -   In the Electrical circuit the ground wire is run along w ith the  phase and neutral wire during the installation. This grounding wire is connected to the common  grounding of the building / premises. For the safety purpose it is essential to ensure the electrical  equipment is two point grounded otherwise it may le ads to accident, if there is any leakage  current  Earth Leakage Circuit Breaker (ELCB)   —   Earth Leakage Circuit Breaker detects grounding  problems and shuts electricity off   to prevent a possible accident.  RCCB :   Residual current circuit breaker   detects   grounding problems and shuts electricity off   to  prevent a possible accident.  Shock Hazard Exposure   –   These approach boundaries are referring to the electrical shock  hazard and NOT the arc flash hazard. WARNING: The arc flash boundary may be greater or  les s than the shock boundary.  Arc Flash : Intense heat and light generated by an electric arc.  Arc Flash Boundary : The boundary within which arc flash PPE is   r equired.  Arc Flash Hazards:   Hazards associated with the arc flash (e.g. radiant and convective energ y,  arc blast   vapours , molten droplets and projectiles).  Arc Rating  The value attributed to materials that describes their performance to exposure to an electrical arc  discharge. The arc rating is expressed in   Cal /cm2 and is derived from the determined value of  the arc thermal performance value (ATPV) or energy of   break open   threshold (EBT) (should a  material system exhibit a   break open   response below the ATPV value). Arc rating is reported as  either ATPV or EBT, w hichever is the lower value.  Arc - rated clothing or equipment indicates that it has been tested for exposure to an electric arc.  Flame resistant clothing without an arc rating has not been tested for exposure to an electric arc.  All arc - rated clothing is a lso flame - resistant.  Arc Thermal Performance Value (ATPV)
--- Page 606 ---
Hindalco Industries Limited  Electrical Safety Standard   Document No.   HI L/ O HS/STD / ES/01  Effective Date   19.03.2020  Review Date   19.03.2022  Page   8   of 58  The incident energy ( Cal /cm2) on a material or a multilayer system of materials that results in a  50% probability that sufficient heat transfer through the tested specimen is predicted to cause th e  onset of a second degree skin burn injury based on the Stoll curve.  Limited Approach Boundary   –   entered by “Qualified” persons or unqualified persons escorted  by qualified person .   ARC Flash suit is required to be worn by all entering into Limited approa ch  Boundary.  Restricted Approach Boundary   –   entered only by qualified persons required to use shock  protection techniques and equipment.   ARC Flash suit is required to be w orn by all entering into  Restricted   approach   Boundary.  Prohibited Approach Boundary   –   entered only by qualified persons requiring same protection as if in  direct contact with live part .   ARC Flash suit is required to be w orn by all entering into Prohibited  approach   Boundary.  NOTE:   Each boundary is a sphere to be observed in all directions (Three dimensional / 360  degrees)  Arc Flash   Suit:   A complete arc - rated clothing and equipment system that covers the entire body,  except for the hands and feet. An arc flash suit may include pant s or overalls, a jacket or a  coverall, and a beekeeper - type hood fitted with a face shield.  Barrier:   An insulating physical obstruction, such as insulating sheeting, an insulating blanket, or  a   Lexan   cover, that is intended to prevent contact with energized electrical conductors and circuit  parts. Barriers may be permanently installed in the equipment, or placed temporarily during  performance of energized work.  Barricade:   A physical obstruction such   as tapes, cones, or A - frame - type wood or metal structures  intended to provide a warning and to prevent unauthorized access to a work area.  6.   Procedure   and Best Practices :  This standard addresses electrical safety   related   work practices,   safety - related   maintenance  requirements,   and other administrative controls for employee   workplaces that are necessary for the  practical safeguarding   of employees relative to the hazards associated with electrical   energy during  activities such as the installation, inspect ion,   operation,   maintenance, and demolition of electric
--- Page 607 ---
Hindalco Industries Limited  Electrical Safety Standard   Document No.   HI L/ O HS/STD / ES/01  Effective Date   19.03.2020  Review Date   19.03.2022  Page   9   of 58  conductors, electric equipment, signalling and communications   conductors and equipment . This  standard   also includes safe work practices for employees performing   other work activities that can  expose t hem to   electrical hazards.  6.1   Formation of Taskforce  6.2   Identification   of Electrical safety   compliance /procedures  6.2.1   Safety certification of E lectrical operated Hand   tools :  A nnexure - 1   used for carrying out   audit of equipment   used in the e ntire   plant.  Key   Principle for safe operation :  1.   No hand tool (company owned & contractor) should be allowed to operate in plant premises  without safety clearance given by electrical workshop.  2. A specific tag no. is issued by electrical workshop & identified by any one   after looking the tag.  3. Each hand tools machine must be connected to input power circuit designed for work only, which  is protected by ELCB/RCCB.  1. Hand tools in defective condition must not be used for maintenance activity.  2. Hand tools that are b roken or require repair should be kept separately and labelled with a warning  notice "DANGER! DO NOT USE FOR WORK!”  3. Hand tools should not be used for purposes other than they are designed for.  4. When power operated tools are designed to accommodate g uards, they shall be equipped with  such guards when in use. Tools shall be used with the correct shield, guard, attachments and  PPE, as recommended by the manufacturer  5. Only non - sparking tools shall be used in an explosive or flammable atmosphere  6. On e should concentrate on the job when using a hand tool.  7.   Playing with hand tools should be strictly prohibited.  8.   Store it in a careful & safe manner when not in use & never leave power tools in work area.  9. Never pull out the plug top from the soc ket in energized condition.
--- Page 608 ---
Hindalco Industries Limited  Electrical Safety Standard   Document No.   HI L/ O HS/STD / ES/01  Effective Date   19.03.2020  Review Date   19.03.2022  Page   10   of 58  10. More than one wire in a plug top is not allowed to operate.  11. Never pull the top by pulling cable of any hand tools.  12. Never remove the ground wire in any manner.  13. Never touch electrical outlets with your fingers   or with objects.  14. Keep the flexible cable away from heat, oil, sharp edges, and moving parts.  15. Replace, don’t attempt to fix cables that have been cut or damaged.  16. Avoid making direct contact with any exposed conductor of the cable.  17. Always keep the hands and the body dry.  18. Do not stand in water or on wet surfaces when operating power hand tools or portable electrical  appliances.  19. Do not use a power hand tool to cut wet or water - soaked building materials, or to repair pipe  leaks  20. Do not use a power hand tool while wearing wet cotton gloves or wet leather gloves  21. Never operate electrical equipment barefooted. Wear rubber - soled or insulated work boots  22. Do not handle or operate electrical tools when your hands are w et or when you are standing on  wet floors .  23 . Use gloves while working with electrical equipment’s.  24 . Recheck & confirm that the electrical circuit has been switched off after work.  25 . All Single phase electrically operated hand tool should have 3 c ore cable if it is not double  insulated. If the hand tool is double insulated than 2 core cable can be used.  26 . No joints in input cable is permitted and maximum length should be 10 Meter.  27 . Industrial connectors/plugs and sockets shall be used for bo th 110v and 240v electrically  operated portable power tools; under no circumstances are domestic 3 pin plugs and sockets  to be used in an industrial or construction environment
--- Page 609 ---
Hindalco Industries Limited  Electrical Safety Standard   Document No.   HI L/ O HS/STD / ES/01  Effective Date   19.03.2020  Review Date   19.03.2022  Page   11   of 58  28.   The Purchasing Policy shall include a requirement to purchase equipment with I S, ANSI, CE or  equivalent marking as per national standards.  29.   Electrically operated portable power   tools shall be double insulated.  30.   All electrically operated tools shall be used within their design limitations  31.   Operators shall be provided with the personal p rotective equipment necessary to protect them  from any specific hazards associated with the use of electrically operated portable power tools,  in accordance with the requirements of the Guidance Note on Personal Protective Equipment.  32.   When operating any el ectrically operated hand tools, workman must ensure that socket is  protected by 30mAmp ELCB/RCCB.  33.   Do Not raise or lower with cable to Electrical hand power tools while working on height also DO  Not hang it on cable.  Procedure for certification:  1.   It   is the responsibility of hand tool owner to shift particular hand tool to the electrical workshop to  check the healthiness & refurbishment details.  2. All the hand tools of the   plant is to be identified as separate ID (W/s Ref no ---- ) no & numbering  to b e done.  3. The Hand tool will be pasted with a specific tag no. (As per location defined below)  4. The hand tool will be checked for its healthiness by competent Electrical Engineer. They will list  out discrepancies & give refurbishment details.  5. Afte r inspection and testing is successful the hand tool will be released to the respective owner  with a certificate, test report.  6. If the hand tool is not found ok in any manner, hand tool will not be released to area till date it  was not fit.  7. In case   of any abnormalities in the equipment the same need to be confirmed to the to the E & I  workshop in specific format (Annexure -   B attached below). The hand tool checked at workshop  and the damage ascertained, whether it can be repaired at site or need to be   sent outside for  repairing.

Hindalco Industries Limited  Electrical Safety Standard   Document No.   HI L/ O HS/STD / ES/01  Effective Date   19.03.2020  Review Date   19.03.2022  Page   12   of 58  8. Other units  procedures are little different.  9. If   the hand tool is completely damaged and can’t be repaired as suggested by OEM (original  equipment manufacturer) service partner, the information for the same will be given to hand tool  owner for purchasing of new hand tool.  10.   A centralized record to be   maintained at electrical workshop of all the hand tools.
